category_summary:
The **SpaceXAI-Anthropic** compute partnership dominated AI discourse, with **xAI** granting **Anthropic** [access to Colossus 1](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-9ff2dfc39258) (220K+ **NVIDIA** GPUs, 300+ MW), and both companies [discussing orbital compute](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-8872e137b8dd) at gigawatt scale. **NVIDIA** [celebrated](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-f00c6ce88988) with 18.4M views. **Ethan Mollick** [offered sharp strategic analysis](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-17617e85d120), calling it a potential blow to **Grok** remaining a frontier model.

- **OpenAI** [announced MRC](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-06f115d87eaa), an open networking protocol for AI training clusters, backed by **AMD**, **Broadcom**, **Intel**, **Microsoft**, and **NVIDIA** — already deployed in production
- **Gary Marcus** [reported **Mira Murati's** testimony](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-2620fb9bffd6) revealing **Sam Altman** was fired for lack of trustworthiness, not AI safety concerns, reframing the Nov 2023 narrative
- **Greg Brockman** [announced **ChatGPT** integration](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-54bd004f1590) for Excel and Google Sheets; **HuggingFace** [launched an agentic robotics app store](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-2e476d2d1f74) with 10,000 robots deployed
- **Levelsio** [flagged **Grok 4.3** API deprecation](/?date=2026-05-07&category=social#item-43f322d3fb94) breaking 30+ production sites, highlighting developer pain around rapid model churn and versioning
category_summary_html:
The SpaceXAI-Anthropic compute partnership dominated AI discourse, with xAI granting Anthropic access to Colossus 1 (220K+ NVIDIA GPUs, 300+ MW), and both companies discussing orbital compute at gigawatt scale. NVIDIA celebrated with 18.4M views. Ethan Mollick offered sharp strategic analysis, calling it a potential blow to Grok remaining a frontier model.

  • OpenAI announced MRC, an open networking protocol for AI training clusters, backed by AMD, Broadcom, Intel, Microsoft, and NVIDIA — already deployed in production
  • Gary Marcus reported Mira Murati's testimony revealing Sam Altman was fired for lack of trustworthiness, not AI safety concerns, reframing the Nov 2023 narrative
  • Greg Brockman announced ChatGPT integration for Excel and Google Sheets; HuggingFace launched an agentic robotics app store with 10,000 robots deployed
  • Levelsio flagged Grok 4.3 API deprecation breaking 30+ production sites, highlighting developer pain around rapid model churn and versioning

We're launching the agentic robotics app store today. Let's democratize AI robotics for all!

300+ a...
items.content:
We're launching the agentic robotics app store today. Let's democratize AI robotics for all!

300+ apps shipped. 10,000 robots in the wild. It used to take weeks from a robotics engineer to build apps, now everyone can do it in hours with ML intern or your favorite neighborhood agent! 

My favorite reachy mini app was built by Joel, a 78yo marketing exec who'd never coded in his life. Personally, I built an office receptionist in two hours last week.

More info to start building here: https://t.co/mQ6YM05lgg

items.title:
Here's exactly what I mean:

On May 15, @xAI Grok will retire grok-4-1-fast-non-reasoning and API re...
items.content:
Here's exactly what I mean:

On May 15, @xAI Grok will retire grok-4-1-fast-non-reasoning and API requests for them will fail

I have 9 days to change the model name in 30+ sites/apps so they keep working

It'd make much more sense to me if they'd just update to the latest model with the same pricing, or let me use a model name like 'latest' that just auto updates

Although actually people's arguments against this make sense regarding pricing:

In this case there is no similar priced "latest" model, pricing goes up from $0.20/input + $0.50/output in Grok 4.1 to $1.25/input + $2.50/output in Grok 4.3

But I'd be fine with that too, I just don't want all my apps/sites to break and keep running forever without me having to change code!

Requests auto-update aliases. Notes pricing increase from $0.20/$0.50 to $1.25/$2.50 (Grok 4.1 → 4.3). Very high engagement (1005 likes, 147K views).
items.reasoning: Highest engagement post in batch. Reveals Grok 4.3 pricing, deprecation timeline, real production impact. Surfaces important API management problem that affects all developers. Confirms Grok 4.3 is live. Contains specific pricing data.

items.title: There are only two honest metrics when it comes to benchmarking intelligence: novelty and efficiency...
items.content:
There are only two honest metrics when it comes to benchmarking intelligence: novelty and efficiency.

You don't need intelligence to solve a known problem (only memory). And you don't need intelligence to solve a problem via brute force. But to solve a novel problem efficiently, intelligence is the only way.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet argues there are only two honest metrics for benchmarking intelligence: novelty and efficiency. You need intelligence only when solving novel problems efficiently - not for known problems (memory) or brute force.
items.reasoning: Important theoretical framework from ARC benchmark creator about measuring intelligence. High engagement (764 likes, 39K views). Directly relevant to AI capability assessment debates.

items.title: A critical question in agent design is “how do we build agentic workflows so humans are given signif...
items.content:
A critical question in agent design is “how do we build agentic workflows so humans are given significant, interesting, or variance-producing decisions as they come up in the work?”

A Claude-run company has no source of competitive advantage compared to other Claude-run firms.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Ethan Mollick argues that agent design must give humans significant decisions, because a Claude-run company has no competitive advantage over other Claude-run companies.
items.reasoning: Excellent strategic insight about the commodification risk of pure AI automation. High engagement (319 likes, 19K views). Important framework for thinking about AI agents in business.

items.title:
👇 This is an incredible admission by Jensen Huang.

Effectively he is saying that AI, for all the hy...
items.content:
👇 This is an incredible admission by Jensen Huang.

Effectively he is saying that AI, for all the hype (some from himself) wasn’t really useful until late 2025.

Let that sink in.

This means practically everything everyone said before was more or less bullshit. 

And it raises a question: *how useful* is it now, and how much it that utility worth?

$10B a year? $50B? 

It can’t just be a little useful to justify the trillions in infrastructure. It has to maybe be the most lucrative technology that ever existed to justify those investments.

So far it ain’t even close to that.

items.title:
🚀 New on the @vllm_project blog: Serving Agentic Workloads at Scale with vLLM x Mooncake.

Agentic t...
items.content:
🚀 New on the @vllm_project blog: Serving Agentic Workloads at Scale with vLLM x Mooncake.

Agentic traces grow to 80K+ tokens with 94%+ reusable prefixes, but local KV caches evict them and cross-instance routing misses them.

By integrating Mooncake Store as a distributed KV cache pool, vLLM gets:

🚀 3.8x higher throughput
⚡ 46x lower P50 TTFT
⏱️ 8.6x lower E2E latency
📈 Cache hit rate 1.7% -> 92.2%
🌐 Scales near-linearly to 60 GB200 GPUs at >95% hit rate

🔥 Powered by a deep collaboration between @Inferact and @KT_Project_AI

📖 Read more: https://t.co/XIRtQ9pYVQ
🧵👇

items.title:
You are brain-dead if you think the CLI killed MCP.

Anthropic just reported 300 million downloads f...
items.content:
You are brain-dead if you think the CLI killed MCP.

Anthropic just reported 300 million downloads for the MCP SDK (up from 100M at the beginning of the year).

The complaint I keep hearing: MCP pollutes your context.

That's a 1-year-old argument. Context pollution is not a protocol problem, but a client one.

None of the agentic tools you use have that issue: Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, etc. All of them implement progressive disclosure and load MCP tools on demand.

MCPs and CLIs complement each other:

• MCP offers OAuth-based authentication, standardized transport, remote access for cloud-hosted agents, and a public registry with thousands of servers.

• CLIs offer Unix-style composition (pipes, stdin/stdout), zero upfront schema cost, and massive training distribution (models have seen far more shell than any structured tool format).

You don't (and shouldn't) pick a side here. You should be using both where they fit.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino arguing that CLI didn't kill MCP - reports 300M MCP SDK downloads (up from 100M early in year). Argues MCP and CLIs are complementary: MCP offers auth/transport/registry, CLI offers Unix composition and training distribution
items.reasoning: Substantive technical analysis of MCP vs CLI debate with hard data (300M downloads). Very high engagement (48K views, 294 likes). Provides balanced framework for thinking about tool integration approaches.

items.title: Complexity is not an intrinsic property of a problem. It's a property of the relationship between a ...
items.content: Complexity is not an intrinsic property of a problem. It's a property of the relationship between a problem and an observer. Once you know the shape of the problem it is no longer complex.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet argues that complexity is relational, not intrinsic - it depends on the observer's knowledge of a problem's structure.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful philosophical insight from a prominent AI researcher about the nature of complexity and intelligence. High engagement (583 likes). Relevant to ongoing debates about AI capability measurement.

items.title: Added a 1500 word mini history to my book on the path to on-policy distillation being a core post-tr...
items.content:
Added a 1500 word mini history to my book on the path to on-policy distillation being a core post-training optimization technique.

The math is fairly simple, seems like the sort of thing that started working as our distributed systems for training got better. It's very remarkable to me that a blog post from @_kevinlu at @thinkymachines is the canonical reference for using the reverse KL distance as an advantage within policy-gradient tools. This switch to distillation objectives within RL setups enables a lot of fun reward shaping ideas.

This also means that on policy distillation was obviously helped in its proliferation by the mass engineering effort in getting RL algorithms right over the last few years.

Lastly, as someone already very familiar with @agarwl_'s early work on generalized knowledge distillation / connection to imitation learning algorithms like DAgger, I recommend reading concurrent work MiniLLM which was technically the first to propose using a policy-gradient-like, on-policy rollout approach for distillation.

The switch from learning from teacher demonstrations to student rollouts seems so obvious in hindsight, where we are with RL hype, but at the time obviously took at bunch of work to get right.

Excited to figure out how to make post-training recipes around this!

items.title: The last sentence in this abstract is really important, in a way that professional programmers will ...
items.content:
The last sentence in this abstract is really important, in a way that professional programmers will immediately recognize: the models favored big single files rather than breaking things into modules.

That means that the code these systems write is going to be really hard to maintain. 

AI code might get written quickly, but especially in new, complex projects, fixing it will be hell.

items.title:
Tencent open-sourced a sandbox that boots AI agents in 60ms. 

CubeSandbox is a self-hosted environm...
items.content:
Tencent open-sourced a sandbox that boots AI agents in 60ms. 

CubeSandbox is a self-hosted environment for running agent code safely.

It boots in under 60ms, roughly 50x faster than typical alternatives.

Each instance uses less than 5MB of memory.

That means over 2,000 isolated environments on a single server.

The speed comes from pre-warmed resource pools and snapshot cloning.

A trimmed Rust runtime with copy-on-write memory keeps overhead tiny.

Security runs deeper than containers usually allow:

> Dedicated guest OS kernel
> KVM-powered microVM isolation
> eBPF network filtering between instances
> No shared-kernel container escape risk

The biggest unlock is drop-in compatibility with the E2B SDK.

Swap one environment variable and migrate from paid services to free infrastructure.

No code changes required.

Already battle-tested in Tencent Cloud production at scale.

Hardware-level isolation for any LLM-generated code finally becomes affordable.

items.title: Last week I gave a talk at AI Dev ’26 by @DeepLearningAI on “AI can’t read PDFs, how do we fix it” ....
items.content:
Last week I gave a talk at AI Dev ’26 by @DeepLearningAI on “AI can’t read PDFs, how do we fix it” .
I’m sharing the slides publicly if others are interested in doing a deep dive into document understanding.

AI agents are going to automate huge amounts of knowledge work, but knowledge work depends on data, a lot of that data is in documents/PDFs, and existing OCR tools suck.

PDFs are a format that is inherently hard to read, and I dive into specific reasons why (tables, layouts), and why frontier VLMs and benchmarks are still insufficient.

Even as agents get better and more general, they need the right tools to read and act over PDFs. They both need this at the data ingest layer, as well as tools they can call on the fly.

Check out the slides: https://t.co/IyaTlQMPlW

We’re building high-quality AI document processing, both with LlamaParse, along with OSS efforts like LiteParse and ParseBench.

If you have a ton of PDFs that you’re hoping to unlock with AI, come talk to us! https://t.co/TsPLwqZ8yg

items.title:
It's all about framing. 

Everyone here on X gave Mark Zuckerberg shit about forcing his employees t...
items.content:
It's all about framing. 

Everyone here on X gave Mark Zuckerberg shit about forcing his employees to surveil themselves to build models. 

That's one framing.

Today I am using a stealth mode company to do the same thing on my computer. Its AI watches EVERYTHING I do. And reads everything coming in from Gmail, Slack, and a bunch of other things. 

Watches what I am doing, learns EVERYTHING I'm doing. 

Here's another framing:

It turns me into a superhuman. Keeps me on task. Shows me what is most important to do next. Has drafts of emails, or docs, or whatever I'm working on all ready for me. 

So now I can chew through work much faster. 

Yeah, it's "surveilling" me. Yeah, it could use that data to train models (it doesn't, that's part of their terms of service) but it COULD be naughty. 

But the tool I'm using helps people work better. And gives the boss much better insight into what a team is doing. 

The framing of "the man is bad, he is watching you and trying to replace you" gets more reach on social media.

The framing of "the man is good, he is watching you, and is trying to help you get more work done" just doesn't get nearly the play. 

When I worked at Microsoft it was after the anti-trust trial where everyone's emails were put into public view. 

So I knew when I worked there that everything I wrote could someday be put in public view and was being stored for future lawsuits. 

If I was running a company I'd force everyone to use this stuff too. Why? Makes a company run better. The company I'm using makes employee satisfaction go up, actually. 

Once they all get over the fact that "the boss is watching me." 

Oh, I am the boss, have now three mouths to feed. Be right back, gonna force them all to surveil themselves. 

By giving them super powers. 

I told you the future is weird. But it really is all about the framing. 

Sorry, be back later, my surveillance system noticed I was on X again instead of doing more important things like answering customers. 

:-)

items.title:
This is just a total lack of creativity on behalf of educators.

Years ago when I was going to colle...
items.content:
This is just a total lack of creativity on behalf of educators.

Years ago when I was going to college my chemistry professor said, on her first day in class, "you are allowed to cheat in my class."

"You can bring the books to my chemistry exams."
"You can bring a calculator or a computer."
"You can write the answers on your arm."

But then she continued.

"All my tests are timed. If you have to look up the right equation to use you will not pass." 

She was right. If you didn't do the homework and study your ass off and really learn the topic you weren't gonna pass.

Asking ChatGPT for the answer is slower than knowing the answer already. 

Education needs a complete overhaul. 

They should start by studying my chemistry teacher. She knew how to teach.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer's main thread version of the chemistry teacher story - arguing education needs overhaul because knowing answers is faster than looking them up with ChatGPT, so timed tests naturally filter for real learning.

"You can bring a calculator or a computer."

"You can write the answers on your arm."

But then she continued.

"All my tests are timed. If you have to look up the right equation to use you will not pass."

She was right. If you didn't do the homework and study your ass off and really learn the topic you weren't gonna pass.

Asking ChatGPT for the answer is slower than knowing the answer already.

Education needs a complete overhaul.

They should start by studying my chemistry teacher. She knew how to teach.

The question is really whether what OpenAI did is ok, not about whether they did it.

items.title:
2) Why agentic workloads break local KV caches:
- Contexts grow to 80K+ tokens by turn 30
- 131:1 in...
items.content:
2) Why agentic workloads break local KV caches:
- Contexts grow to 80K+ tokens by turn 30
- 131:1 input-to-output ratio
- A single session's KV cache can be multiple GB
- Routers migrate sessions, so the next instance starts cold
🔥 Our open-source trace: https://t.co/SYuNMC6lGP
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM explains why agentic workloads break local KV caches: contexts grow 80K+ tokens, 131:1 input-to-output ratio, multi-GB KV caches per session, router migration causes cold starts
items.reasoning: Clear articulation of a key infrastructure challenge for agentic AI workloads, with open-source trace data shared
items.summary_html: vLLM explains why agentic workloads break local KV caches: contexts grow 80K+ tokens, 131:1 input-to-output ratio, multi-GB KV caches per session, router migration causes cold starts
items.content_html:
2) Why agentic workloads break local KV caches:

  • Contexts grow to 80K+ tokens by turn 30
  • 131:1 input-to-output ratio
  • A single session's KV cache can be multiple GB
  • Routers migrate sessions, so the next instance starts cold

🔥 Our open-source trace: https://t.co/SYuNMC6lGP

items.title:
@LuizaJarovsky This is just a total lack of creativity on behalf of educators.  

Years ago when I w...
items.content:
@LuizaJarovsky This is just a total lack of creativity on behalf of educators.  

Years ago when I was going to college my chemistry professor said, on her first day in class, "you are allowed to cheat in my class."  

"You can bring the books to my chemistry exams." 

"You can bring a calculator or a computer." 

"You can write the answers on your arm."  

But then she continued.  

"All my tests are timed. If you have to look up the right equation to use you will not pass."   

She was right. 

If you didn't do the homework and study your ass off and really learn the topic you weren't gonna pass.  Asking ChatGPT for the answer is slower than knowing the answer already.   

Education needs a complete overhaul.   

They should start by studying my chemistry teacher. She knew how to teach.

items.title:
3) The fix: a cluster-wide KV cache pool built on Mooncake Store.

vLLM workers move KV blocks direc...
items.content:
3) The fix: a cluster-wide KV cache pool built on Mooncake Store.

vLLM workers move KV blocks directly between GPU HBM and a shared DRAM/SSD pool via GPUDirect RDMA. SM-free, zero-copy, fully async. Plugs into the existing KVConnector and composes with PD disaggregation.

items.title:
Uni-1 is now available as an API!

This is a multimodal reasoning model that resolves creative inten...
items.content:
Uni-1 is now available as an API!

This is a multimodal reasoning model that resolves creative intent before generating a single pixel.

With the API, you can now wire Uni-1 into a product, a custom pipeline, or any code you build at production scale.

You can have Uni-1 pretty much anywhere you can run code.

This model *thinks* and *reflects* before it starts generating pixels. ← This is very different from what we are used to.

When you hand it a brief, a sketch, or a reference board, the model works out what you are trying to make (your intent) before anything else.

In practice, this gives you outputs that are much more consistent with your references and instructions.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino announces Uni-1 multimodal reasoning model now available as API. The model thinks and reflects on creative intent before generating pixels, producing outputs more consistent with references.
items.reasoning: New model API launch with reasoning-before-generation approach for image creation. Moderate engagement. Represents emerging trend of reasoning in image generation.

items.title:
Neural networks are about to become as predictable as physics. 

Most people treat neural networks l...
items.content:
Neural networks are about to become as predictable as physics. 

Most people treat neural networks like a black box. You train, you tune, you pray.

A new paper argues that's about to change. 

The authors claim deep learning is getting its own scientific theory, and they're calling it learning mechanics.

The idea is simple. Neural network training behaves like a physical system, with predictable patterns and laws.

Five research threads back this up:

> Toy models that can be solved exactly
> Limits that expose how features form
> Scaling laws with clean equations
> Hyperparameters that transfer across model sizes
> Universal patterns across different setups

Each one mirrors something in classical physics.

What this unlocks is concrete. 

Tuning small models and predicting how big ones behave. 

Eliminating guesswork from training recipes.

It frames mechanistic interpretability as the biology of networks, while learning mechanics plays the role of physics.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal discusses paper arguing neural network training follows predictable physical laws - 'learning mechanics' as a theory enabling predictions about large model behavior from small model experiments
items.reasoning: Interesting theoretical paper discussion about making neural network training more predictable. Could have major practical implications if validated
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal discusses paper arguing neural network training follows predictable physical laws - 'learning mechanics' as a theory enabling predictions about large model behavior from small model experiments

How are your favorite LLMs or other AIs built?

Data. Books, magazine articles, videos, social media posts, and much more.

Today I talk with @partymesis who runs one of the companies that collects that data and sells it to the AI companies.

It's insightful, because he runs the largest network of people who produce new data to train future AIs.

https://t.co/3LCt7ebHC7

items.title: @yasminekho Steve Jobs got an attitude because he saw how wrong everyone was at the earliest stages ...
items.content:
@yasminekho Steve Jobs got an attitude because he saw how wrong everyone was at the earliest stages of starting Apple.

Remember, Wozniak BEGGED HP to build a personal computer. Woz told me he was turned down FIVE TIMES by HP.

Why?

Their market research showed that no one wanted a personal computer. So they refused to make one.

It's a pattern I've seen repeated MANY TIMES over the years.

Mercedes had everything that Elon had in its Silicon Valley lab (I gave a speech to the engineers who built it). The German execs refused to ship a touch screen and an always updating computer along with a software-defined car.

Why?

Their consumer research misled the execs. People told them they wanted buttons instead of a touch screen. I did my own research on that topic, going around America, and they were right. People loved buttons.

But in a Robotaxi world buttons are stupid. Elon just saw that 15 years ago. He was thinking of Robotaxi way back then and autonomy and saw the consumer was changing.

Go back to 2007. So many people told me "I'll never buy an iPhone, need a phone with a keyboard."

Jobs knew that new customers would show up if he did something better and showed it to them.

He learned this from Woz, who wanted a personal computer for himself. He was tired of sneaking into big companies in the middle of the night to program computers.

Turns out he wasn't the only one.

When Jobs died a lot of people, me included, over indexed on Jobs as an a$$hole.

All people who start companies are really a$$holes, if you look at it right.

They are trying to get us all to change.

Humans hate change. So we call them names until we realize they were right to try to get us all to change.

items.title:
The early signs are always there. These startups are making sure we catch them. 

#NVIDIAInception s...
items.content:
The early signs are always there. These startups are making sure we catch them. 

#NVIDIAInception startups are turning sensors, satellites, and advanced models into early warning systems across some of the hardest problems to solve:
 
🛰️ Wildfire detection and damage assessment from space and on the ground
⛈️ Hyperlocal weather forecasts that traditional radar can't match
🚧 Real-time hazard detection for workers and vehicles in complex environments
 
The common thread: AI that spots risk before it becomes harm.

items.title: @3lectricBrawl For years Microsoft's stock was flat while its profits were growing. People inside th...
items.content:
@3lectricBrawl For years Microsoft's stock was flat while its profits were growing. People inside the company wondered why.

It's belief. 

Most people today don't believe Elon will have the best robotaxi. Will have the best Semi. Will have the best humanoid robot. 

Taking advantage of lack of belief as an investor is one of the biggest gifts around. But you gotta do your homework to have conviction to go against the crowd.

items.title:
@avrldotdev Kodak invented the digital camera. 
Wozniak begged HP to make the first personal compute...
items.content:
@avrldotdev Kodak invented the digital camera. 
Wozniak begged HP to make the first personal computer. Turned him down five times, he told me.
AltaVista "owned" search before Google came along.
Nokia "owned" smartphones before Apple came along.

I could go all day. Big companies are hamstrung by constituents. Who told me that? The guy who ran Internet Explorer at Microsoft.

items.title:
"My AI agents are bankrupting me."

I think that often when I see the bills that https://t.co/8L5xph...
items.content:
"My AI agents are bankrupting me."

I think that often when I see the bills that https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ are running up. $150 a day sometimes to have my agents read my AI lists here on X. 

But then I remember what a Stanford education costs. $70,000 a year. 

And my agents aren't just going to Stanford (it reads everything Stanford publishes) but are reading the smartest humans alive. 

They are getting smarter. My agents know more about AI than your agents do. 

This was actually the pitch to @blevlabs when I convinced him to work with me. 

He built my agent. So his agents are getting smarter too. 

We can rent you my agent. What will you get back? 

My agent already has a million posts from the smartest people in its database. 

Unless you have your OpenClaw doing the same, and have had it running for months, your agent doesn't have the same. 

Oh, and it read my book in two minutes. 

Your agents don't have the PDF to feed in my book. 

And Brayden's agents keep getting smarter over time. 

There's a reason why one of San Francisco's best startup residencies invited him in. 

They see the value in an expensive set of agents who are learning faster than any other. 

Oh, and want to catch up? The only way is to use my lists here on X. Why? They are the most complete collection of smart people on a variety of topics that exist here on X. 

In other words, I built the AI equivilent of Stanford University. 

The lists are free. But your agents are behind. 

And if you want mine because they are ahead you gotta pay. 

Just like if you want a Stanford student to work for you you gotta pay.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble's long post about spending $150/day on AI agents that read his curated X lists, claiming to have built 'AI equivalent of Stanford University' with 1M posts from smartest people. Pitching agent rental as a service.
items.reasoning: Most detailed post in the batch about AI agent economics and architecture. Reveals real costs ($150/day) and scale (1M posts). However, highly self-promotional and the Stanford comparison is hyperbolic. Decent engagement (9K views).
items.summary_html: Scoble's long post about spending $150/day on AI agents that read his curated X lists, claiming to have built 'AI equivalent of Stanford University' with 1M posts from smartest people. Pitching agent rental as a service.

items.title:
New fun thing I did to secure my VPS even further

I installed @Cloudflare Tunnel, many of you recom...
items.content:
New fun thing I did to secure my VPS even further

I installed @Cloudflare Tunnel, many of you recommended me this

I already had 443 inbound firewall limited to Cloudflare's IP range, but this is even better

Cloudflare Tunnel is outbound, which means it connects from your server to Cloudflare, and keeps the connection active, then if someone opens your site, Cloudflare sends you the package via the tunnel and your server responds

Then you can block ALL inbound traffic on your firewall (in my case the Hetzner firewall in the dashboard), so now NOBODY can ever access my server, only Cloudflare and Tailscale (which is my own subnet which just my server and my laptop on it)

You can just ask AI to set it up on the server etc., very easy
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio explains Cloudflare Tunnel setup for VPS security - outbound-only connections, blocking all inbound traffic, combined with Tailscale.
items.reasoning: High engagement (1689 likes, 184K views) practical security tutorial. Mentions 'ask AI to set it up' but primarily infra/security content.
items.summary_html: Levelsio explains Cloudflare Tunnel setup for VPS security - outbound-only connections, blocking all inbound traffic, combined with Tailscale.
items.content_html:
New fun thing I did to secure my VPS even further

I installed @Cloudflare Tunnel, many of you recommended me this

I already had 443 inbound firewall limited to Cloudflare's IP range, but this is even better

Cloudflare Tunnel is outbound, which means it connects from your server to Cloudflare, and keeps the connection active, then if someone opens your site, Cloudflare sends you the package via the tunnel and your server responds

Then you can block ALL inbound traffic on your firewall (in my case the Hetzner firewall in the dashboard), so now NOBODY can ever access my server, only Cloudflare and Tailscale (which is my own subnet which just my server and my laptop on it)

You can just ask AI to set it up on the server etc., very easy

items.content:
@SJW75 I saw a system that can teach anyone to read fast. 

I'm not too worried. 

One taught me to take apart an electric motor in real time on top of the motor. 

I didn't need to read a book, or watch a video. 

Times are a changing. We need new skills.

I tell my kids "who will be more interesting at a party? Someone who does things or someone who just cheats their way through life?"

Or, another way to put it, let's say you are going to Paris in a couple of weeks on vacation. 

You can cheat and use AI to translate.

Or you can put in a little work and learn a little French.

Which one will get you a better experience in Paris? 

I know. The one who learns some French will have a lot better time, even though their phone can translate everything for them already.

I know. The one who learns some French will have a lot better time, even though their phone can translate everything for them already.

items.title:
@AlloyLauncher @avrldotdev I was with Nokia's CEO as Steve Jobs announced the new iPhone.

It was hu...
items.content:
@AlloyLauncher @avrldotdev I was with Nokia's CEO as Steve Jobs announced the new iPhone.

It was hubris. Pure and simple. 

He gave me shit for three years about my iPhone. "How's your phone with a shitty camera and shitty radios" he'd ask me. 

"It's better than your phone, at least I can use this shitty phone." 

I still have a drawer full of Nokia's. 

And yes I know about Microsoft's role in putting a final nail in the coffin. 

I hung out with the Microsoft mobile phone team long before Steve Jobs built a phone. 

Hubris there too.

items.title: @MuzafferKal_ @LuizaJarovsky Great writing can be done by AI. My AI at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ alrea...
items.content:
@MuzafferKal_ @LuizaJarovsky Great writing can be done by AI. My AI at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ already writes just as well as I can about the AI news of the day. It can read a lot more than I can too. Read my book in two minutes that took a year and a half to write.

Again, what makes a great human in 50 years? 

Someone who can teach a computer something new. 

What's the best way in 50 years to talk to a computer? Well, you will have a brain computer interface on by then. 

Which means you think, and the computer can talk back to you in your brain.

Who will be best at thinking? 

Those who learn to write. Writing is the best way to train your brain on something. 

People who are going to school should reevaluate why they are going to school? 

Is it to cheat their way through life? See if that serves you well. 

Writing is the best way to train your brain to think. 

And, yeah, it's a real problem. 

But it really comes down to a lack of creativity by the education system itself.

Oh, and AI can teach you to write a lot better than any English class I've ever been to. 

Another way to look at it? Let's say you are going to Paris on vacation in a couple of weeks. 

Yeah, you can "cheat" your way through that and use your phone to translate everyone you meet. 

But I've done that already. 

You'll have a much better experience when you put a little effort into learning some French.

items.title:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal:

**Prep reading for the embeddings and vector systems interview**

...
items.content:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal:

**Prep reading for the embeddings and vector systems interview**

This curriculum provides a comprehensive foundation for understanding modern vector systems, beginning with the evolution of word representations from early static embedding methods to advanced transformer-based architectures.

Learners will study the architectural shift from recurrent networks to attention mechanisms, progressing through pre-training strategies like BERT and the refinement of sentence embeddings for semantic similarity and contrastive learning.

The coursework then bridges the gap between language understanding and scalable information retrieval by examining late-interaction models, dense retrieval techniques, and visual-language alignment.

Finally, the curriculum covers the technical challenges of billion-scale search, detailing state-of-the-art developments in approximate nearest neighbor algorithms, quantization methods, and graph-based indexing structures designed for efficient large-scale deployment.

https://t.co/8lMkouHZaA

items.title:
@dailygoat @investorDC01 Same as teaching. 

I often take speaking gigs where I don't know anything ...
items.content:
@dailygoat @investorDC01 Same as teaching. 

I often take speaking gigs where I don't know anything about the topic. 

Ran a panel last week on responsible AI. I don't really know anything about the topic, or didn't before last week. 

Taking that assignment forced me to learn. 

AI taught me what I needed to know. 

And my panel got top marks. Little did they know that a week before I really didn't know much. 

Same with all my books. I had to work my ass off to learn to write a book on a topic.

items.title:
Here’s the problem with most AI setups:

→ Data is sent to the cloud
→ Models process remotely
→ Dec...
items.content:
Here’s the problem with most AI setups:

→ Data is sent to the cloud
→ Models process remotely
→ Decisions come back with delay

That delay is latency.

And in operations, latency slows performance, limits responsiveness, and impacts business value.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon explains the latency problem with cloud-based AI processing for real-time operations.
items.reasoning: Part of promotional thread but articulates a real technical problem in AI deployment clearly.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon explains the latency problem with cloud-based AI processing for real-time operations.
items.content_html:
Here’s the problem with most AI setups:

→ Data is sent to the cloud

→ Models process remotely

→ Decisions come back with delay

That delay is latency.

And in operations, latency slows performance, limits responsiveness, and impacts business value.

items.title:
The insight that stood out:

HR decisions depend on layered context.

Not just general rules, but pr...
items.content:
The insight that stood out:

HR decisions depend on layered context.

Not just general rules, but priorities.

For example:

→ National legislation sets the baseline
→ Company policy often overrides in practice
→ Edge cases change everything

If your AI is not trained on this, it will give the wrong answers confidently.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon explains HR AI needs layered context - national legislation, company policy, and edge cases.

Move AI closer to where decisions happen.

That’s how AI moves from analysis to execution.

items.content:
In real operations, speed isn’t a feature.
It’s the requirement.

Think about:
→ Manufacturing lines
→ Logistics networks
→ Smart infrastructure

These environments depend on ultra-low latency and real-time responsiveness.

They don’t need insights later.
They need decisions now.

items.title:
HR teams are navigating an overload of AI tools.

The real problem is not access, it is clarity.

Mo...
items.content:
HR teams are navigating an overload of AI tools.

The real problem is not access, it is clarity.

Most teams don’t define:

→ What problems AI should solve
→ What it should not touch
→ How decisions are actually made internally

Without that, even the best tools underperform.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon notes HR teams struggle with AI tool overload and lack clarity on what AI should/shouldn't do.
items.reasoning: Common but valid point about enterprise AI adoption challenges.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon notes HR teams struggle with AI tool overload and lack clarity on what AI should/shouldn't do.
items.content_html:
HR teams are navigating an overload of AI tools.

The real problem is not access, it is clarity.

Most teams don’t define:

→ What problems AI should solve

→ What it should not touch

→ How decisions are actually made internally

Without that, even the best tools underperform.

items.content:
All general-purpose online communities in all social networks have died: LinkedIn groups, Google+, Facebook Groups, X Communities.

The only example of a successful online general-purpose community is Reddit.

The only example of a successful online general-purpose community is Reddit.

If someone invents a working formula for an online community, it will be worth billions.

items.title:
@Cassius_Reborn @3lectricBrawl In 10 years...

Are there more or less autonomous vehicles on the roa...
items.content:
@Cassius_Reborn @3lectricBrawl In 10 years...

Are there more or less autonomous vehicles on the road?

Are there more or less electric semis on the road?

Are there more or less batteries on people's homes?

Are there more or less humanoid robots in people's homes? 

I've done the homework to know that many of those will come from Tesla. 

Never look at P/E ratios. They don't matter.

What does?

Relevance to the future.

And Tesla is highly relevant to the future. 

If you only care about P/E ratios you would have bought Nokia the day that Apple came out with the first iPhone.

items.title:
@burby_geek @Nick_Davidov My son lives in New York and is 31. Never drives. 

Soon no one will need ...
items.content:
@burby_geek @Nick_Davidov My son lives in New York and is 31. Never drives. 

Soon no one will need to drive.

Only reason to learn to drive now is merely for fun or because the world hasn't switched over yet. It will. Fast. Just watch. 

If my kid wants to race a car he'll learn to drive. So far he hasn't shown any interest. Maybe someday he will. Then he will learn. 

Or he'll race robots. @A2RLeague already does and their robots beat the humans last year. :-)

Siri was launched in my home. That went on to...
items.content:
@schinsly @sama It's one of many mistakes I've made. 

Siri was launched in my home. That went on to be Apple's most profitable acquisition of all time. 

I was the 79th user of Instagram. 

Uber was invented right in front of me. 

I launched thousands of companies in my life. 

Didn't invest in any of them.

items.title:
@LuisRebelo I know, right? 

My boss in the consumer electronics store took away cash registers. Why...
items.content:
@LuisRebelo I know, right? 

My boss in the consumer electronics store took away cash registers. Why? He wanted us to stop making mistakes. Forced us to learn math. 

Paid us on commission. So if you were faster at math you got paid more. 

I learned to do it VERY fast.

And having a few cheats helped while learning. Doesn't every kid have a times table? Yeah. :-)
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble sharing anecdote about learning mental math by removing cash registers, analogizing to AI learning tools
items.reasoning: Personal anecdote loosely tied to AI learning
